name: Cooking
description: Cooking techniques, ingredients, and world dishes

questions.q: Which nut is the main ingredient in French macarons?

source.notes: Ground almonds plus meringue form the shells.

questions.q: Feta is a type of which food?

source.notes: Brined curd cheese traditionally from Greece, made from sheep or sheep/goat milk.

questions.q: Kimchi, a spicy fermented vegetable dish, originates from which country?

source.notes: Typically made with napa cabbage and chili pepper paste (gochugaru).

questions.q: Gnocchi are small dumplings typically made from which ingredient, originating in which country?

source.notes: 'Gnocchi di patate' are the most common.

questions.q: Pancetta is an Italian version of which cured meat?

source.notes: Pork belly cured with salt and spices.

questions.q: The squeaky cheese Halloumi originates on which Mediterranean island?

source.notes: Has a high melting point, perfect for grilling.

questions.q: Which vegetable is added to eggs to make a Spanish tortilla (tortilla de patatas)?

source.notes: Fried potatoes and onions bound in egg.

questions.q: The expensive spice saffron comes from the stigmas of which flower?

source.notes: It takes about 150,000 flowers to produce 1 kg of saffron.

questions.q: Kippers are a cured, smoked variety of which fish?

source.notes: Split from tail to head, gutted, salted, and cold-smoked.

questions.q: Blinis are small savory pancakes traditionally from which country?

source.notes: Often served with caviar, sour cream, or smoked salmon.

questions.q: The Christmas cake 'Panettone' originates from which country?

source.notes: Originated in Milan; sweet, tall, and studded with dried fruit.

questions.q: Cavolo Nero is a type of what leafy green?

source.notes: Also called Tuscan kale or lacinato kale.

questions.q: The French cooking term 'en papillote' means wrapping food in what before baking?

source.notes: French for 'in parchment'; steams the food in its own juices.

questions.q: The Spanish cheese Manchego is traditionally made from the milk of which animal?

source.notes: From sheep of the Manchega breed in La Mancha, Spain.

questions.q: Wasabi, often served with sushi, is also known as what?

source.notes: Most 'wasabi' served outside Japan is actually dyed horseradish.

questions.q: Blueberries are native to which continent?

source.notes: One of the few fruits native to North America.

questions.q: The chili-based piri-piri (peri-peri) style of cooking originated in which country?

source.notes: Developed by Portuguese settlers using African bird's eye chilis.

questions.q: The Irish dish 'colcannon' is mashed potato mixed with which other vegetable?

source.notes: Often served with butter and bacon on Halloween in Ireland.

questions.q: Before the 17th century, carrots were predominantly which color?

source.notes: Orange carrots were bred in the Netherlands in the 1600s.

questions.q: Which slow-cooking French technique involves cooking meat for a long time in liquid at a low temperature?

source.notes: Great for tough cuts like oxtail or lamb shank.

questions.q: Sous vide cooking involves sealing food in a bag and cooking it in what?

source.notes: French for 'under vacuum'.

questions.q: 'Nduja' is a spreadable spicy type of what?

source.notes: From Calabria, Italy; made with pork and Calabrian chilis.
